source_paper_title: Single-Server Queues with State-Dependent Hawkes Arrivals

Section 8.3 (page 29–30) and Conclusion, Section 9 (page 31).

Since we show numerically that the square-root rule is not always satisfactory for Hawkes queues, one natural direction is to theoretically verify the numerical finding and identify the correct asymptotic staffing rule.
title: Derive the correct heavy-traffic staffing rule for Hawkes arrival single-server queues
background_latex:
Consider a single-server FIFO queue with constant service rate \(\mu>0\). Jobs arrive according to a stationary Hawkes point process \(N(t)\) on \(\mathbb{R}\) with background intensity \(\lambda_0>0\) and excitation kernel \(h:\mathbb{R}_+\to\mathbb{R}_+\). The Hawkes process has conditional intensity
\[\lambda(t)=\lambda_0+\sum_{t_i<t} h(t-t_i),\]
where \(\{t_i\}\) are arrival times. Let
\[m \equiv \int_0^\infty h(u)\,du,\]
and assume \(m<1\) so the Hawkes process is stable and stationary. Let \(V\) be a positive job-size (workload) random variable with \(\mathbb{E}[V]=1\); the service time of a job of size \(V\) is \(V/\mu\). Let \(W_\infty(\mu)\) denote the steady-state workload (unfinished work) under service rate \(\mu\). The paper studies a staffing/capacity-sizing objective of the form
\[f(\mu)=h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)] + c(\mu),\]
where \(h_0>0\) is a congestion-penalty coefficient and \(c(\mu)\) is a staffing cost (e.g., linear \(c(\mu)=\kappa\mu\) in heavy-traffic staffing heuristics). The classical “square-root staffing rule” for renewal-arrival queues (e.g., GI/GI/1 under linear staffing cost) asserts that the optimizer \(\mu^*\) behaves as
\[\mu^* = \lambda + O(\sqrt{\lambda}) \quad \text{as } \lambda\to\infty,\]
where \(\lambda\) is the mean arrival rate. For a Hawkes process, the mean arrival rate is \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\). The paper reports numerical experiments indicating that, in Hawkes/GI/1 queues where the increase of \(\lambda\) is driven by stronger self-excitation (e.g., \(m\to 1\) with fixed \(\lambda_0\)), the square-root rule appears to fail and \((\mu^*-\lambda)/\sqrt{\lambda}\) increases markedly with \(\lambda\).
problem_definition_latex: Determine the correct asymptotic scaling (staffing rule) for the optimal service rate \(\mu^*\) in a Hawkes/GI/1 queue in a heavy-traffic regime where the mean arrival rate \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\) becomes large (e.g., via \(m\to 1\)). In particular, characterize the asymptotic order of \(\mu^*-\lambda\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (and/or as \(m\to 1\)) for the optimal staffing problem with linear staffing cost, as suggested by the numerical finding that the classical square-root scaling \(\mu^*=\lambda+O(\sqrt{\lambda})\) may not hold for Hawkes/GI/1 queues.
latex:
Consider a single-server FIFO queue with constant service rate \(\mu>0\). Jobs arrive according to a stationary Hawkes point process \(N(t)\) on \(\mathbb{R}\) with background intensity \(\lambda_0>0\) and excitation kernel \(h:\mathbb{R}_+\to\mathbb{R}_+\). The Hawkes process has conditional intensity
\[\lambda(t)=\lambda_0+\sum_{t_i<t} h(t-t_i),\]
where \(\{t_i\}\) are arrival times. Let
\[m \equiv \int_0^\infty h(u)\,du,\]
and assume \(m<1\) so the Hawkes process is stable and stationary. Let \(V\) be a positive job-size (workload) random variable with \(\mathbb{E}[V]=1\); the service time of a job of size \(V\) is \(V/\mu\). Let \(W_\infty(\mu)\) denote the steady-state workload (unfinished work) under service rate \(\mu\). The paper studies a staffing/capacity-sizing objective of the form
\[f(\mu)=h_0\,\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)] + c(\mu),\]
where \(h_0>0\) is a congestion-penalty coefficient and \(c(\mu)\) is a staffing cost (e.g., linear \(c(\mu)=\kappa\mu\) in heavy-traffic staffing heuristics). The classical “square-root staffing rule” for renewal-arrival queues (e.g., GI/GI/1 under linear staffing cost) asserts that the optimizer \(\mu^*\) behaves as
\[\mu^* = \lambda + O(\sqrt{\lambda}) \quad \text{as } \lambda\to\infty,\]
where \(\lambda\) is the mean arrival rate. For a Hawkes process, the mean arrival rate is \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\). The paper reports numerical experiments indicating that, in Hawkes/GI/1 queues where the increase of \(\lambda\) is driven by stronger self-excitation (e.g., \(m\to 1\) with fixed \(\lambda_0\)), the square-root rule appears to fail and \((\mu^*-\lambda)/\sqrt{\lambda}\) increases markedly with \(\lambda\).

Determine the correct asymptotic scaling (staffing rule) for the optimal service rate \(\mu^*\) in a Hawkes/GI/1 queue in a heavy-traffic regime where the mean arrival rate \(\lambda=\lambda_0/(1-m)\) becomes large (e.g., via \(m\to 1\)). In particular, characterize the asymptotic order of \(\mu^*-\lambda\) as \(\lambda\to\infty\) (and/or as \(m\to 1\)) for the optimal staffing problem with linear staffing cost, as suggested by the numerical finding that the classical square-root scaling \(\mu^*=\lambda+O(\sqrt{\lambda})\) may not hold for Hawkes/GI/1 queues.
open_problem_quote: Since we show numerically that the square-root rule is not always satisfactory for Hawkes queues, one natural direction is to theoretically verify the numerical finding and identify the correct asymptotic staffing rule.
subject_classification: Queueing theory and heavy-traffic asymptotics for self-exciting arrivals
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for an asymptotic characterization of an optimal-policy scaling, not a finite combinatorial statement. While simulations can suggest violations of square-root scaling, they do not constitute a mathematical counterexample to a precisely stated theorem because the correct asymptotic regime and regularity conditions must be formalized.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no clear automatically checkable fitness function for a proposed asymptotic rule without first specifying a rigorous limiting regime and proving bounds on \(\mathbb{E}[W_\infty(\mu)]\). Automated code search could explore heuristics via simulation, but simulation-based evaluation cannot certify the asymptotic optimality or derive proofs.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
